<div class="onlyprint" id="jb-print-docs-body">
<h1>Tracing Walkthrough</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">
<div id="jb-print-toc">
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div id="searchbox"></div>
<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="tracing-walkthrough">
<h1>Tracing Walkthrough<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing-walkthrough" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<div class="cell docutils container">
<div class="cell_input docutils container">
<div class="highlight-ipython3 notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="kn">import</span> <span class="nn">os</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_HANDLER"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"langchain"</span>

<span class="c1">## Uncomment this if using hosted setup.</span>

<span class="c1"># os.environ["LANGCHAIN_ENDPOINT"] = "https://langchain-api-gateway-57eoxz8z.uc.gateway.dev" </span>

<span class="c1">## Uncomment this if you want traces to be recorded to "my_session" instead of default.</span>

<span class="c1"># os.environ["LANGCHAIN_SESSION"] = "my_session"  </span>

<span class="c1">## Better to set this environment variable in the terminal</span>
<span class="c1">## Uncomment this if using hosted version. Replace "my_api_key" with your actual API Key.</span>

<span class="c1"># os.environ["LANGCHAIN_API_KEY"] = "my_api_key"  </span>

<span class="kn">import</span> <span class="nn">langchain</span>
<span class="kn">from</span> <span class="nn">langchain.agents</span> <span class="kn">import</span> <span class="n">Tool</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">initialize_agent</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">load_tools</span>
<span class="kn">from</span> <span class="nn">langchain.chat_models</span> <span class="kn">import</span> <span class="n">ChatOpenAI</span>
<span class="kn">from</span> <span class="nn">langchain.llms</span> <span class="kn">import</span> <span class="n">OpenAI</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell docutils container">
<div class="cell_input docutils container">
<div class="highlight-ipython3 notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="c1"># Agent run with tracing. Ensure that OPENAI_API_KEY is set appropriately to run this example.</span>

<span class="n">llm</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">OpenAI</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">temperature</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mi">0</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="n">tools</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">load_tools</span><span class="p">([</span><span class="s2">"llm-math"</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">llm</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="n">llm</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell docutils container">
<div class="cell_input docutils container">
<div class="highlight-ipython3 notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="n">agent</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">initialize_agent</span><span class="p">(</span>
    <span class="n">tools</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">llm</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">agent</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s2">"zero-shot-react-description"</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">verbose</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="kc">True</span>
<span class="p">)</span>

<span class="n">agent</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">run</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">"What is 2 raised to .123243 power?"</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell_output docutils container">
<div class="output stream highlight-myst-ansi notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="-Color -Color-Bold">&gt; Entering new AgentExecutor chain...</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green"> I need to use a calculator to solve this.</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Action: Calculator</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Action Input: 2^.123243</span>
Observation: <span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Cyan">Answer: 1.0891804557407723</span>

Thought:<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green"> I now know the final answer.</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Final Answer: 1.0891804557407723</span>

<span class="-Color -Color-Bold">&gt; Finished chain.</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
<div class="output text_plain highlight-myst-ansi notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span>'1.0891804557407723'
</pre></div>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell docutils container">
<div class="cell_input docutils container">
<div class="highlight-ipython3 notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="c1"># Agent run with tracing using a chat model</span>
<span class="n">agent</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">initialize_agent</span><span class="p">(</span>
    <span class="n">tools</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">ChatOpenAI</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">temperature</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mi">0</span><span class="p">),</span> <span class="n">agent</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s2">"chat-zero-shot-react-description"</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">verbose</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="kc">True</span>
<span class="p">)</span>

<span class="n">agent</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">run</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">"What is 2 raised to .123243 power?"</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell_output docutils container">
<div class="output stream highlight-myst-ansi notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="-Color -Color-Bold">&gt; Entering new AgentExecutor chain...</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Question: What is 2 raised to .123243 power?</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Thought: I need a calculator to solve this problem.</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Action:</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">```</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">{</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">  "action": "calculator",</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">  "action_input": "2^0.123243"</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">}</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">```</span>

Observation: calculator is not a valid tool, try another one.
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">I made a mistake, I need to use the correct tool for this question.</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Action:</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">```</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">{</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">  "action": "calculator",</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">  "action_input": "2^0.123243"</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">}</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">```</span>

Observation: calculator is not a valid tool, try another one.
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">I made a mistake, the tool name is actually "calc" instead of "calculator".</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Action:</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">```</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">{</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">  "action": "calc",</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">  "action_input": "2^0.123243"</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">}</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">```</span>

Observation: calc is not a valid tool, try another one.
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">I made another mistake, the tool name is actually "Calculator" instead of "calc".</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Action:</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">```</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">{</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">  "action": "Calculator",</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">  "action_input": "2^0.123243"</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">}</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">```</span>

Observation: <span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Cyan">Answer: 1.0891804557407723</span>

Thought:<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">The final answer is 1.0891804557407723.</span>
<span class="-Color -Color-Bold -Color-Bold-Green">Final Answer: 1.0891804557407723</span>

<span class="-Color -Color-Bold">&gt; Finished chain.</span>
</pre></div>
</div>

</pre></div>
</div>

<div class="onlyprint" id="jb-print-docs-body">
<h1>Tracing</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">
<div id="jb-print-toc">
<div>
<h2> Contents </h2>
</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">
<ul class="visible nav section-nav flex-column">
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#tracing-walkthrough">Tracing Walkthrough</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#changing-sessions">Changing Sessions</a></li>
</ul>
</nav>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div id="searchbox"></div>
<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="tracing">
<h1>Tracing<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>By enabling tracing in your LangChain runs, you’ll be able to more effectively visualize, step through, and debug your chains and agents.</p>
<p>First, you should install tracing and set up your environment properly.
You can use either a locally hosted version of this (uses Docker) or a cloud hosted version (in closed alpha).
If you’re interested in using the hosted platform, please fill out the form <a class="reference external" href="https://forms.gle/tRCEMSeopZf6TE3b6">here</a>.</p>
<ul class="simple">
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/local_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Locally Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/hosted_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Cloud Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
</ul>
<section id="tracing-walkthrough">
<h2>Tracing Walkthrough<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing-walkthrough" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>When you first access the UI, you should see a page with your tracing sessions.
An initial one “default” should already be created for you.
A session is just a way to group traces together.
If you click on a session, it will take you to a page with no recorded traces that says “No Runs.”
You can create a new session with the new session form.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/homepage.png"/></p>
<p>If we click on the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">default</span></code> session, we can see that to start we have no traces stored.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/default_empty.png"/></p>
<p>If we now start running chains and agents with tracing enabled, we will see data show up here.
To do so, we can run <a class="reference internal" href="tracing/agent_with_tracing.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">this notebook</span></a> as an example.
After running it, we will see an initial trace show up.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/first_trace.png"/></p>
<p>From here we can explore the trace at a high level by clicking on the arrow to show nested runs.
We can keep on clicking further and further down to explore deeper and deeper.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore.png"/></p>
<p>We can also click on the “Explore” button of the top level run to dive even deeper.
Here, we can see the inputs and outputs in full, as well as all the nested traces.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_trace.png"/></p>
<p>We can keep on exploring each of these nested traces in more detail.
For example, here is the lowest level trace with the exact inputs/outputs to the LLM.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_llm.png"/></p>
</section>
<section id="changing-sessions">
<h2>Changing Sessions<a class="headerlink" href="#changing-sessions" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<ol class="arabic simple">
<li><p>To initially record traces to a session other than <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">"default"</span></code>, you can set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable to the name of the session you want to record to:</p></li>
</ol>
<div class="highlight-python notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="kn">import</span> <span class="nn">os</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_HANDLER"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"langchain"</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_SESSION"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"my_session"</span> <span class="c1"># Make sure this session actually exists. You can create a new session in the UI.</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
<ol class="arabic simple" start="2">
<li><p>To switch sessions mid-script or mid-notebook, do NOT set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable. Instead: <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">langchain.set_tracing_callback_manager(session_name="my_session")</span></code></p></li>
</ol>
</section>
</section>

<div class="onlyprint" id="jb-print-docs-body">
<h1>Tracing</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">
<div id="jb-print-toc">
<div>
<h2> Contents </h2>
</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">
<ul class="visible nav section-nav flex-column">
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#tracing-walkthrough">Tracing Walkthrough</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#changing-sessions">Changing Sessions</a></li>
</ul>
</nav>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div id="searchbox"></div>
<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="tracing">
<h1>Tracing<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>By enabling tracing in your LangChain runs, you’ll be able to more effectively visualize, step through, and debug your chains and agents.</p>
<p>First, you should install tracing and set up your environment properly.
You can use either a locally hosted version of this (uses Docker) or a cloud hosted version (in closed alpha).
If you’re interested in using the hosted platform, please fill out the form <a class="reference external" href="https://forms.gle/tRCEMSeopZf6TE3b6">here</a>.</p>
<ul class="simple">
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/local_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Locally Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/hosted_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Cloud Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
</ul>
<section id="tracing-walkthrough">
<h2>Tracing Walkthrough<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing-walkthrough" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>When you first access the UI, you should see a page with your tracing sessions.
An initial one “default” should already be created for you.
A session is just a way to group traces together.
If you click on a session, it will take you to a page with no recorded traces that says “No Runs.”
You can create a new session with the new session form.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/homepage.png"/></p>
<p>If we click on the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">default</span></code> session, we can see that to start we have no traces stored.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/default_empty.png"/></p>
<p>If we now start running chains and agents with tracing enabled, we will see data show up here.
To do so, we can run <a class="reference internal" href="tracing/agent_with_tracing.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">this notebook</span></a> as an example.
After running it, we will see an initial trace show up.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/first_trace.png"/></p>
<p>From here we can explore the trace at a high level by clicking on the arrow to show nested runs.
We can keep on clicking further and further down to explore deeper and deeper.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore.png"/></p>
<p>We can also click on the “Explore” button of the top level run to dive even deeper.
Here, we can see the inputs and outputs in full, as well as all the nested traces.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_trace.png"/></p>
<p>We can keep on exploring each of these nested traces in more detail.
For example, here is the lowest level trace with the exact inputs/outputs to the LLM.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_llm.png"/></p>
</section>
<section id="changing-sessions">
<h2>Changing Sessions<a class="headerlink" href="#changing-sessions" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<ol class="arabic simple">
<li><p>To initially record traces to a session other than <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">"default"</span></code>, you can set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable to the name of the session you want to record to:</p></li>
</ol>
<div class="highlight-python notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="kn">import</span> <span class="nn">os</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_HANDLER"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"langchain"</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_SESSION"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"my_session"</span> <span class="c1"># Make sure this session actually exists. You can create a new session in the UI.</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
<ol class="arabic simple" start="2">
<li><p>To switch sessions mid-script or mid-notebook, do NOT set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable. Instead: <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">langchain.set_tracing_callback_manager(session_name="my_session")</span></code></p></li>
</ol>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Tracing</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">
<div id="jb-print-toc">
<div>
<h2> Contents </h2>
</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">
<ul class="visible nav section-nav flex-column">
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#tracing-walkthrough">Tracing Walkthrough</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#changing-sessions">Changing Sessions</a></li>
</ul>
</nav>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div id="searchbox"></div>
<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="tracing">
<h1>Tracing<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>By enabling tracing in your LangChain runs, you’ll be able to more effectively visualize, step through, and debug your chains and agents.</p>
<p>First, you should install tracing and set up your environment properly.
You can use either a locally hosted version of this (uses Docker) or a cloud hosted version (in closed alpha).
If you’re interested in using the hosted platform, please fill out the form <a class="reference external" href="https://forms.gle/tRCEMSeopZf6TE3b6">here</a>.</p>
<ul class="simple">
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/local_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Locally Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/hosted_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Cloud Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
</ul>
<section id="tracing-walkthrough">
<h2>Tracing Walkthrough<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing-walkthrough" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>When you first access the UI, you should see a page with your tracing sessions.
An initial one “default” should already be created for you.
A session is just a way to group traces together.
If you click on a session, it will take you to a page with no recorded traces that says “No Runs.”
You can create a new session with the new session form.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/homepage.png"/></p>
<p>If we click on the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">default</span></code> session, we can see that to start we have no traces stored.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/default_empty.png"/></p>
<p>If we now start running chains and agents with tracing enabled, we will see data show up here.
To do so, we can run <a class="reference internal" href="tracing/agent_with_tracing.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">this notebook</span></a> as an example.
After running it, we will see an initial trace show up.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/first_trace.png"/></p>
<p>From here we can explore the trace at a high level by clicking on the arrow to show nested runs.
We can keep on clicking further and further down to explore deeper and deeper.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore.png"/></p>
<p>We can also click on the “Explore” button of the top level run to dive even deeper.
Here, we can see the inputs and outputs in full, as well as all the nested traces.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_trace.png"/></p>
<p>We can keep on exploring each of these nested traces in more detail.
For example, here is the lowest level trace with the exact inputs/outputs to the LLM.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_llm.png"/></p>
</section>
<section id="changing-sessions">
<h2>Changing Sessions<a class="headerlink" href="#changing-sessions" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<ol class="arabic simple">
<li><p>To initially record traces to a session other than <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">"default"</span></code>, you can set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable to the name of the session you want to record to:</p></li>
</ol>
<div class="highlight-python notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="kn">import</span> <span class="nn">os</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_HANDLER"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"langchain"</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_SESSION"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"my_session"</span> <span class="c1"># Make sure this session actually exists. You can create a new session in the UI.</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
<ol class="arabic simple" start="2">
<li><p>To switch sessions mid-script or mid-notebook, do NOT set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable. Instead: <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">langchain.set_tracing_callback_manager(session_name="my_session")</span></code></p></li>
</ol>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Tracing</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">
<div id="jb-print-toc">
<div>
<h2> Contents </h2>
</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">
<ul class="visible nav section-nav flex-column">
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#tracing-walkthrough">Tracing Walkthrough</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#changing-sessions">Changing Sessions</a></li>
</ul>
</nav>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div id="searchbox"></div>
<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="tracing">
<h1>Tracing<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>By enabling tracing in your LangChain runs, you’ll be able to more effectively visualize, step through, and debug your chains and agents.</p>
<p>First, you should install tracing and set up your environment properly.
You can use either a locally hosted version of this (uses Docker) or a cloud hosted version (in closed alpha).
If you’re interested in using the hosted platform, please fill out the form <a class="reference external" href="https://forms.gle/tRCEMSeopZf6TE3b6">here</a>.</p>
<ul class="simple">
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/local_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Locally Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/hosted_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Cloud Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
</ul>
<section id="tracing-walkthrough">
<h2>Tracing Walkthrough<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing-walkthrough" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>When you first access the UI, you should see a page with your tracing sessions.
An initial one “default” should already be created for you.
A session is just a way to group traces together.
If you click on a session, it will take you to a page with no recorded traces that says “No Runs.”
You can create a new session with the new session form.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/homepage.png"/></p>
<p>If we click on the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">default</span></code> session, we can see that to start we have no traces stored.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/default_empty.png"/></p>
<p>If we now start running chains and agents with tracing enabled, we will see data show up here.
To do so, we can run <a class="reference internal" href="tracing/agent_with_tracing.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">this notebook</span></a> as an example.
After running it, we will see an initial trace show up.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/first_trace.png"/></p>
<p>From here we can explore the trace at a high level by clicking on the arrow to show nested runs.
We can keep on clicking further and further down to explore deeper and deeper.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore.png"/></p>
<p>We can also click on the “Explore” button of the top level run to dive even deeper.
Here, we can see the inputs and outputs in full, as well as all the nested traces.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_trace.png"/></p>
<p>We can keep on exploring each of these nested traces in more detail.
For example, here is the lowest level trace with the exact inputs/outputs to the LLM.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_llm.png"/></p>
</section>
<section id="changing-sessions">
<h2>Changing Sessions<a class="headerlink" href="#changing-sessions" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<ol class="arabic simple">
<li><p>To initially record traces to a session other than <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">"default"</span></code>, you can set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable to the name of the session you want to record to:</p></li>
</ol>
<div class="highlight-python notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="kn">import</span> <span class="nn">os</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_HANDLER"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"langchain"</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_SESSION"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"my_session"</span> <span class="c1"># Make sure this session actually exists. You can create a new session in the UI.</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
<ol class="arabic simple" start="2">
<li><p>To switch sessions mid-script or mid-notebook, do NOT set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable. Instead: <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">langchain.set_tracing_callback_manager(session_name="my_session")</span></code></p></li>
</ol>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Tracing</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">
<div id="jb-print-toc">
<div>
<h2> Contents </h2>
</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">
<ul class="visible nav section-nav flex-column">
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#tracing-walkthrough">Tracing Walkthrough</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#changing-sessions">Changing Sessions</a></li>
</ul>
</nav>

<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="tracing">
<h1>Tracing<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>By enabling tracing in your LangChain runs, you’ll be able to more effectively visualize, step through, and debug your chains and agents.</p>
<p>First, you should install tracing and set up your environment properly.
You can use either a locally hosted version of this (uses Docker) or a cloud hosted version (in closed alpha).
If you’re interested in using the hosted platform, please fill out the form <a class="reference external" href="https://forms.gle/tRCEMSeopZf6TE3b6">here</a>.</p>
<ul class="simple">
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/local_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Locally Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
<li><p><a class="reference internal" href="tracing/hosted_installation.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">Cloud Hosted Setup</span></a></p></li>
</ul>
<section id="tracing-walkthrough">
<h2>Tracing Walkthrough<a class="headerlink" href="#tracing-walkthrough" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>When you first access the UI, you should see a page with your tracing sessions.
An initial one “default” should already be created for you.
A session is just a way to group traces together.
If you click on a session, it will take you to a page with no recorded traces that says “No Runs.”
You can create a new session with the new session form.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/homepage.png"/></p>
<p>If we click on the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">default</span></code> session, we can see that to start we have no traces stored.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/default_empty.png"/></p>
<p>If we now start running chains and agents with tracing enabled, we will see data show up here.
To do so, we can run <a class="reference internal" href="tracing/agent_with_tracing.html"><span class="doc std std-doc">this notebook</span></a> as an example.
After running it, we will see an initial trace show up.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/first_trace.png"/></p>
<p>From here we can explore the trace at a high level by clicking on the arrow to show nested runs.
We can keep on clicking further and further down to explore deeper and deeper.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore.png"/></p>
<p>We can also click on the “Explore” button of the top level run to dive even deeper.
Here, we can see the inputs and outputs in full, as well as all the nested traces.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_trace.png"/></p>
<p>We can keep on exploring each of these nested traces in more detail.
For example, here is the lowest level trace with the exact inputs/outputs to the LLM.</p>
<p><img alt="" src="_images/explore_llm.png"/></p>
</section>
<section id="changing-sessions">
<h2>Changing Sessions<a class="headerlink" href="#changing-sessions" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<ol class="arabic simple">
<li><p>To initially record traces to a session other than <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">"default"</span></code>, you can set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable to the name of the session you want to record to:</p></li>
</ol>
<div class="highlight-python notranslate"><div class="highlight"><pre><span></span><span class="kn">import</span> <span class="nn">os</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_HANDLER"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"langchain"</span>
<span class="n">os</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">environ</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">"LANGCHAIN_SESSION"</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"my_session"</span> <span class="c1"># Make sure this session actually exists. You can create a new session in the UI.</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
<ol class="arabic simple" start="2">
<li><p>To switch sessions mid-script or mid-notebook, do NOT set the <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">LANGCHAIN_SESSION</span></code> environment variable. Instead: <code class="docutils literal notranslate"><span class="pre">langchain.set_tracing_callback_manager(session_name="my_session")</span></code></p></li>
</ol>
</section>
</section>
</article>

</body>
</html># Deployments

So you've made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?

This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.

What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.

## [Streamlit](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template)

This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.

## [Gradio (on Hugging Face)](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template)

This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a "Bring-Your-Own-Token" approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver's [excellent examples](https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert).

## [Beam](https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering)

This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with [Beam](https://beam.cloud).

It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.

## [Vercel](https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain)

A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.

## [SteamShip](https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/)
This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.

## [Langchain-serve](https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve)
This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to [Jina](https://docs.jina.ai/). Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.<!DOCTYPE html>

<h1>Deployments</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">

</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">

<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#beam">Beam</a></li>

<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="deployments">
<h1>Deployments<a class="headerlink" href="#deployments" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>So you’ve made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?</p>
<p>This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.</p>
<p>What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.</p>
<section id="streamlit">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template">Streamlit</a><a class="headerlink" href="#streamlit" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.</p>
</section>
<section id="gradio-on-hugging-face">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a><a class="headerlink" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a “Bring-Your-Own-Token” approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver’s <a class="reference external" href="https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert">excellent examples</a>.</p>
</section>
<section id="beam">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering">Beam</a><a class="headerlink" href="#beam" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with <a class="reference external" href="https://beam.cloud">Beam</a>.</p>
<p>It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.</p>
</section>
<section id="vercel">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain">Vercel</a><a class="headerlink" href="#vercel" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.</p>
</section>
<section id="steamship">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/">SteamShip</a><a class="headerlink" href="#steamship" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.</p>
</section>
<section id="langchain-serve">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a><a class="headerlink" href="#langchain-serve" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to <a class="reference external" href="https://docs.jina.ai/">Jina</a>. Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.</p>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Deployments</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">

<h2> Contents </h2>
</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">

<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#beam">Beam</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#vercel">Vercel</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#steamship">SteamShip</a></li>

<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="deployments">
<h1>Deployments<a class="headerlink" href="#deployments" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>So you’ve made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?</p>
<p>This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.</p>
<p>What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.</p>
<section id="streamlit">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template">Streamlit</a><a class="headerlink" href="#streamlit" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.</p>
</section>
<section id="gradio-on-hugging-face">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a><a class="headerlink" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a “Bring-Your-Own-Token” approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver’s <a class="reference external" href="https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert">excellent examples</a>.</p>
</section>
<section id="beam">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering">Beam</a><a class="headerlink" href="#beam" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with <a class="reference external" href="https://beam.cloud">Beam</a>.</p>
<p>It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.</p>
</section>
<section id="vercel">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain">Vercel</a><a class="headerlink" href="#vercel" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.</p>
</section>
<section id="steamship">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/">SteamShip</a><a class="headerlink" href="#steamship" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.</p>
</section>
<section id="langchain-serve">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a><a class="headerlink" href="#langchain-serve" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to <a class="reference external" href="https://docs.jina.ai/">Jina</a>. Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.</p>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Deployments</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">

</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">

<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#beam">Beam</a></li>

<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="deployments">
<h1>Deployments<a class="headerlink" href="#deployments" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>So you’ve made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?</p>
<p>This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.</p>
<p>What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.</p>
<section id="streamlit">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template">Streamlit</a><a class="headerlink" href="#streamlit" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.</p>
</section>
<section id="gradio-on-hugging-face">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a><a class="headerlink" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a “Bring-Your-Own-Token” approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver’s <a class="reference external" href="https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert">excellent examples</a>.</p>
</section>
<section id="beam">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering">Beam</a><a class="headerlink" href="#beam" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with <a class="reference external" href="https://beam.cloud">Beam</a>.</p>
<p>It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.</p>
</section>
<section id="vercel">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain">Vercel</a><a class="headerlink" href="#vercel" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.</p>
</section>
<section id="steamship">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/">SteamShip</a><a class="headerlink" href="#steamship" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.</p>
</section>
<section id="langchain-serve">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a><a class="headerlink" href="#langchain-serve" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to <a class="reference external" href="https://docs.jina.ai/">Jina</a>. Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.</p>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Deployments</h1>

<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="deployments">
<h1>Deployments<a class="headerlink" href="#deployments" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>So you’ve made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?</p>
<p>This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.</p>
<p>What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.</p>
<section id="streamlit">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template">Streamlit</a><a class="headerlink" href="#streamlit" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.</p>
</section>
<section id="gradio-on-hugging-face">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a><a class="headerlink" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a “Bring-Your-Own-Token” approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver’s <a class="reference external" href="https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert">excellent examples</a>.</p>
</section>
<section id="beam">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering">Beam</a><a class="headerlink" href="#beam" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with <a class="reference external" href="https://beam.cloud">Beam</a>.</p>
<p>It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.</p>
</section>
<section id="vercel">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain">Vercel</a><a class="headerlink" href="#vercel" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.</p>
</section>
<section id="steamship">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/">SteamShip</a><a class="headerlink" href="#steamship" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.</p>
</section>
<section id="langchain-serve">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a><a class="headerlink" href="#langchain-serve" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to <a class="reference external" href="https://docs.jina.ai/">Jina</a>. Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.</p>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<li class="toctree-l3"><a class="reference internal" href="modules/models/text_embedding/examples/cohere.html">Cohere</a></li>

<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="deployments">
<h1>Deployments<a class="headerlink" href="#deployments" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>So you’ve made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?</p>
<p>This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.</p>
<p>What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.</p>
<section id="streamlit">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template">Streamlit</a><a class="headerlink" href="#streamlit" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.</p>
</section>
<section id="gradio-on-hugging-face">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a><a class="headerlink" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a “Bring-Your-Own-Token” approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver’s <a class="reference external" href="https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert">excellent examples</a>.</p>
</section>
<section id="beam">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering">Beam</a><a class="headerlink" href="#beam" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with <a class="reference external" href="https://beam.cloud">Beam</a>.</p>
<p>It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.</p>
</section>
<section id="vercel">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain">Vercel</a><a class="headerlink" href="#vercel" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.</p>
</section>
<section id="steamship">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/">SteamShip</a><a class="headerlink" href="#steamship" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.</p>
</section>
<section id="langchain-serve">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a><a class="headerlink" href="#langchain-serve" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to <a class="reference external" href="https://docs.jina.ai/">Jina</a>. Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.</p>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Deployments</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">
<div id="jb-print-toc">
<div>
<h2> Contents </h2>
</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">

<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#beam">Beam</a></li>

<div id="searchbox"></div>
<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="deployments">
<h1>Deployments<a class="headerlink" href="#deployments" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>So you’ve made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?</p>
<p>This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.</p>
<p>What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.</p>
<section id="streamlit">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template">Streamlit</a><a class="headerlink" href="#streamlit" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.</p>
</section>
<section id="gradio-on-hugging-face">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a><a class="headerlink" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a “Bring-Your-Own-Token” approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver’s <a class="reference external" href="https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert">excellent examples</a>.</p>
</section>
<section id="beam">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering">Beam</a><a class="headerlink" href="#beam" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with <a class="reference external" href="https://beam.cloud">Beam</a>.</p>
<p>It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.</p>
</section>
<section id="vercel">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain">Vercel</a><a class="headerlink" href="#vercel" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.</p>
</section>
<section id="steamship">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/">SteamShip</a><a class="headerlink" href="#steamship" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.</p>
</section>
<section id="langchain-serve">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a><a class="headerlink" href="#langchain-serve" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to <a class="reference external" href="https://docs.jina.ai/">Jina</a>. Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.</p>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Deployments</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">
<div id="jb-print-toc">
<div>
<h2> Contents </h2>
</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">
<ul class="visible nav section-nav flex-column">
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#streamlit">Streamlit</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#beam">Beam</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#vercel">Vercel</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#steamship">SteamShip</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a></li>
</ul>

<div id="searchbox"></div>
<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="deployments">
<h1>Deployments<a class="headerlink" href="#deployments" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>So you’ve made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?</p>
<p>This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.</p>
<p>What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.</p>
<section id="streamlit">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template">Streamlit</a><a class="headerlink" href="#streamlit" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.</p>
</section>
<section id="gradio-on-hugging-face">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a><a class="headerlink" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a “Bring-Your-Own-Token” approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver’s <a class="reference external" href="https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert">excellent examples</a>.</p>
</section>
<section id="beam">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering">Beam</a><a class="headerlink" href="#beam" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with <a class="reference external" href="https://beam.cloud">Beam</a>.</p>
<p>It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.</p>
</section>
<section id="vercel">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain">Vercel</a><a class="headerlink" href="#vercel" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.</p>
</section>
<section id="steamship">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/">SteamShip</a><a class="headerlink" href="#steamship" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.</p>
</section>
<section id="langchain-serve">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a><a class="headerlink" href="#langchain-serve" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to <a class="reference external" href="https://docs.jina.ai/">Jina</a>. Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.</p>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Deployments</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">

</div>

<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#beam">Beam</a></li>

<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="deployments">
<h1>Deployments<a class="headerlink" href="#deployments" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>So you’ve made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?</p>
<p>This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.</p>
<p>What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.</p>
<section id="streamlit">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template">Streamlit</a><a class="headerlink" href="#streamlit" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.</p>
</section>
<section id="gradio-on-hugging-face">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a><a class="headerlink" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a “Bring-Your-Own-Token” approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver’s <a class="reference external" href="https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert">excellent examples</a>.</p>
</section>
<section id="beam">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering">Beam</a><a class="headerlink" href="#beam" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with <a class="reference external" href="https://beam.cloud">Beam</a>.</p>
<p>It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.</p>
</section>
<section id="vercel">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain">Vercel</a><a class="headerlink" href="#vercel" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.</p>
</section>
<section id="steamship">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/">SteamShip</a><a class="headerlink" href="#steamship" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.</p>
</section>
<section id="langchain-serve">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a><a class="headerlink" href="#langchain-serve" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to <a class="reference external" href="https://docs.jina.ai/">Jina</a>. Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.</p>
</section>
</section>
</article>

<h1>Deployments</h1>
<!-- Table of contents -->
<div id="print-main-content">
<div id="jb-print-toc">
<div>
<h2> Contents </h2>
</div>
<nav aria-label="Page">
<ul class="visible nav section-nav flex-column">
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#streamlit">Streamlit</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#beam">Beam</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#vercel">Vercel</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#steamship">SteamShip</a></li>
<li class="toc-h2 nav-item toc-entry"><a class="reference internal nav-link" href="#langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a></li>
</ul>

<div id="searchbox"></div>
<article class="bd-article" role="main">
<section id="deployments">
<h1>Deployments<a class="headerlink" href="#deployments" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h1>
<p>So you’ve made a really cool chain - now what? How do you deploy it and make it easily sharable with the world?</p>
<p>This section covers several options for that.
Note that these are meant as quick deployment options for prototypes and demos, and not for production systems.
If you are looking for help with deployment of a production system, please contact us directly.</p>
<p>What follows is a list of template GitHub repositories aimed that are intended to be
very easy to fork and modify to use your chain.
This is far from an exhaustive list of options, and we are EXTREMELY open to contributions here.</p>
<section id="streamlit">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-streamlit-template">Streamlit</a><a class="headerlink" href="#streamlit" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how to deploy a LangChain with Streamlit.
It implements a chatbot interface.
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Streamlit platform.</p>
</section>
<section id="gradio-on-hugging-face">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain-gradio-template">Gradio (on Hugging Face)</a><a class="headerlink" href="#gradio-on-hugging-face" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with Gradio.
It implements a chatbot interface, with a “Bring-Your-Own-Token” approach (nice for not wracking up big bills).
It also contains instructions for how to deploy this app on the Hugging Face platform.
This is heavily influenced by James Weaver’s <a class="reference external" href="https://huggingface.co/JavaFXpert">excellent examples</a>.</p>
</section>
<section id="beam">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/slai-labs/get-beam/tree/main/examples/langchain-question-answering">Beam</a><a class="headerlink" href="#beam" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repo serves as a template for how deploy a LangChain with <a class="reference external" href="https://beam.cloud">Beam</a>.</p>
<p>It implements a Question Answering app and contains instructions for deploying the app as a serverless REST API.</p>
</section>
<section id="vercel">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/homanp/vercel-langchain">Vercel</a><a class="headerlink" href="#vercel" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>A minimal example on how to run LangChain on Vercel using Flask.</p>
</section>
<section id="steamship">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/steamship-core/steamship-langchain/">SteamShip</a><a class="headerlink" href="#steamship" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository contains LangChain adapters for Steamship, enabling LangChain developers to rapidly deploy their apps on Steamship.
This includes: production ready endpoints, horizontal scaling across dependencies, persistant storage of app state, multi-tenancy support, etc.</p>
</section>
<section id="langchain-serve">
<h2><a class="reference external" href="https://github.com/jina-ai/langchain-serve">Langchain-serve</a><a class="headerlink" href="#langchain-serve" title="Permalink to this headline">#</a></h2>
<p>This repository allows users to serve local chains and agents as RESTful, gRPC, or Websocket APIs thanks to <a class="reference external" href="https://docs.jina.ai/">Jina</a>. Deploy your chains &amp; agents with ease and enjoy independent scaling, serverless and autoscaling APIs, as well as a Streamlit playground on Jina AI Cloud.</p>
</section>
</section>
</article>
